teamObjective.explanation: This is the opening lead with a very weak, non-trump hand. Leading the lowest single gives my partner the best chance to win the trick with a higher card, while I keep my slightly stronger 4-6 for future plays.

partnerBelief.summary: Partner has 3 unknown cards and no public signals yet. They might hold a higher single or trump to take this trick, but they could also be weak.

opponentBelief.summary: Opponents each have 3 unknown cards and are likely to beat a low single if they can, but no specific distribution or strength can be inferred from public events alone.

actionRationale.primaryReason: Leading the spade 3 is the weakest card, so it minimizes the value I give away and maximizes the chance that partner can win the trick with any higher single or trump. It also preserves the 4, 5, and 6 for later tricks.

whyNotAlternatives.reason: The 4 is only slightly stronger than the 3 and still very unlikely to win the trick outright; playing it now would leave me with an even weaker remaining hand.
The 5 is one of my better low cards; leading it wastes a card that might help me win or control a future low trick, with little added chance of winning this lead.

The 6 is my highest non-trump card and my best chance to win a low trick later; leading it now risks wasting my only meaningful winner on an opening trick where opponents or partner likely have higher cards.

risks: An opponent may beat the low lead and then lead a combination I cannot match.
Partner may not have a higher card, so the lead may pass to the opponent team anyway.
I have no trump cards, so I cannot regain control easily if I lose the lead.
riskAssessment.mitigation: By leading the absolute lowest card, I minimize the strength I reveal and retain my modest 4-6 cards, giving me the best available options if I get another turn.
